text: This is a video of a patient with left indirect inguinal hernia. In this video, I'll be showing the critical view of myopic tineal orifice as described by Dr. Diaz and Dr. Felix. After the indirect sac was completely reduced, we typically have the nine steps of the critical view of the myopectenial orifice printed in the OR and we go through them one by one to make sure that we have it achieved prior to placing the mesh, which is step number nine. And it also helps us educate the residents.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the pubic tubercle across the midline and Cooper ligament, extending towards the contralateral Cooper ligament for large direct hernias. The direct hernia is rolled out, and unusual fat is moved in the Heselbeck triangle. Dissection is performed between the Cooper ligament and bladder to facilitate flat placement of the mesh's medial and inferior edge towards the space of Retzius. Dissection continues between the Cooper ligament and iliac vein to identify the femoral orifice and rule out a femoral hernia. The indirect sac and peritoneum are dissected to parietalize the cord elements, ensuring they lie flat, while visualizing the psoas muscle and iliac vessels. The sac and peritoneum are pulled upward without moving the cord elements, and dissection occurs between the cord elements to avoid missing a tail of the sac. A cord lipoma is identified and reduced, located usually lateral to the cord elements, and not confused with lymph nodes. The peritoneum is dissected lateral to the cord elements beyond the anterior superior spine, sweeping it back inferiorly behind the mesh's inferior border.
The surgeon places a 16 by 12 centimeter self-fixating pro grip mesh into the surgical site, ensuring it adapts to the contour of the space and the cord elements without undue memory.
